verses.text: 'My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?' — the cry of one unanswered by day or night. David's anguish here reaches beyond himself to Christ on the cross, who made these very words His own. The Saviour bore the forsaking that we might never be forsaken.

'My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?' — the cry of one unanswered by day or night. David's anguish here reaches beyond himself to Christ on the cross, who made these very words His own. The Saviour bore the forsaking that we might never be forsaken.

Yet, the sufferer says, 'thou art holy,' and the fathers trusted and were delivered. Faith holds to God's character in the dark. When feeling says He has fled, the soul clings to what it knows of His faithfulness.

Yet, the sufferer says, 'thou art holy,' and the fathers trusted and were delivered. Faith holds to God's character in the dark. When feeling says He has fled, the soul clings to what it knows of His faithfulness.

Yet, the sufferer says, 'thou art holy,' and the fathers trusted and were delivered. Faith holds to God's character in the dark. When feeling says He has fled, the soul clings to what it knows of His faithfulness.

'I am a worm, and no man,' despised and mocked, while scoffers sneer, 'He trusted on the LORD; let him deliver him.' These taunts were echoed at Calvary. The Christ who saved others was jeered as unable to save Himself.

'I am a worm, and no man,' despised and mocked, while scoffers sneer, 'He trusted on the LORD; let him deliver him.' These taunts were echoed at Calvary. The Christ who saved others was jeered as unable to save Himself.

'I am a worm, and no man,' despised and mocked, while scoffers sneer, 'He trusted on the LORD; let him deliver him.' These taunts were echoed at Calvary. The Christ who saved others was jeered as unable to save Himself.

He recalls that God drew him from the womb and made him hope at his mother's breast, and pleads, 'be not far from me; for trouble is near.' Lifelong care is his ground of prayer. The God who kept us from the first will not abandon us at the worst.

He recalls that God drew him from the womb and made him hope at his mother's breast, and pleads, 'be not far from me; for trouble is near.' Lifelong care is his ground of prayer. The God who kept us from the first will not abandon us at the worst.

He recalls that God drew him from the womb and made him hope at his mother's breast, and pleads, 'be not far from me; for trouble is near.' Lifelong care is his ground of prayer. The God who kept us from the first will not abandon us at the worst.
Strong enemies surround him like the bulls of Bashan, gaping with open mouths. He is hemmed in by raging foes. So the powers of the world closed in upon the suffering Christ.
Strong enemies surround him like the bulls of Bashan, gaping with open mouths. He is hemmed in by raging foes. So the powers of the world closed in upon the suffering Christ.

'I am poured out like water,' his bones out of joint, his strength dried up, brought 'into the dust of death.' This is the very anguish of crucifixion, foretold a thousand years before. The Son of God truly entered death's dust for us.

'I am poured out like water,' his bones out of joint, his strength dried up, brought 'into the dust of death.' This is the very anguish of crucifixion, foretold a thousand years before. The Son of God truly entered death's dust for us.

'They pierced my hands and my feet,' they stare and gloat, and 'they part my garments... and cast lots upon my vesture.' Every detail was fulfilled at the cross. The Spirit painted Calvary in prophecy long before the nails were forged.

'They pierced my hands and my feet,' they stare and gloat, and 'they part my garments... and cast lots upon my vesture.' Every detail was fulfilled at the cross. The Spirit painted Calvary in prophecy long before the nails were forged.

He cries to God not to be far, to deliver his soul from the sword and the lion's mouth. The depth of suffering drives him to urgent prayer. Even in the darkest extremity, the sufferer keeps calling on God.

He cries to God not to be far, to deliver his soul from the sword and the lion's mouth. The depth of suffering drives him to urgent prayer. Even in the darkest extremity, the sufferer keeps calling on God.

He cries to God not to be far, to deliver his soul from the sword and the lion's mouth. The depth of suffering drives him to urgent prayer. Even in the darkest extremity, the sufferer keeps calling on God.

Then the tone turns: 'I will declare thy name unto my brethren,' for God 'hath not despised... the affliction of the afflicted.' Suffering gives way to praise. The risen Christ proclaims the Father's name among the people He redeemed.

Then the tone turns: 'I will declare thy name unto my brethren,' for God 'hath not despised... the affliction of the afflicted.' Suffering gives way to praise. The risen Christ proclaims the Father's name among the people He redeemed.

The meek shall eat and be satisfied, and 'all the ends of the world shall remember and turn unto the LORD,' for the kingdom is His. The cross bears worldwide fruit. From the Saviour's anguish flows the gathering of the nations to God.

The meek shall eat and be satisfied, and 'all the ends of the world shall remember and turn unto the LORD,' for the kingdom is His. The cross bears worldwide fruit. From the Saviour's anguish flows the gathering of the nations to God.

All who go down to the dust shall bow before Him, and a coming generation shall be told that 'he hath done this.' The finished work is proclaimed to ages unborn. 'It is finished' echoes here: He has done it, and we live by it.
